elements.sent-2411-001: Dark matter is theorised to form massive haloes, which could be further condensed into so-called spikes when a black hole grows at the centre of such a halo.
elements.sent-2411-002: The existence of these spikes is instrumental for several dark matter detection schemes such as indirect detection and imprints on gravitational wave inspirals, but all previous work on their formation has been (semi-)analytical.
elements.sent-2411-003: We present fully numerically simulated cold dark matter spikes using the SWIFT code.
elements.sent-2411-004: Based on these results, we propose a simple empirical density profile - dependent on only a single mass-ratio parameter between the black hole and total mass - for dark matter spikes grown in Hernquist profiles.
elements.sent-2411-005: We find that the radius of the spike scales differently compared to theoretical predictions, and show a depletion of the outer halo that is significant for high mass-ratio systems.
elements.sent-2411-006: We critically assess approximations of the spike as used in the field, show that our profile significantly deviates, and contextualise the potential influence for future dark matter detections by simulating binary black hole inspirals embedded in our profile.
elements.sent-2411-007: One of the biggest mysteries in modern cosmology is the origin of Dark Matter (DM).
elements.sent-2411-008: While never directly detected, it has been indirectly observed in systems ranging from small galaxies to clusters of galaxies, with more work underway focused on direct detection or solving theoretical challenges (for an overview, see \citealt{PDG ).}
elements.sent-2411-009: The most recent Planck satellite survey found, assuming standard $\Lambda$CDM cosmology, that $26.1\%$ of the total mass-energy of the universe consists of this DM, which is $83.9\%$ of the total matter density \citep{Planck:2018vyg .}
elements.sent-2411-010: DM is theorised to form small density perturbations in the early universe, which stop expanding after sufficient growth and collapse into a spherical halo \citep*{NFW,Bullock:2017xww .\footnote{If this collapse is too fast, a BH forms instead \citep{Green:2020jor .}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: While this work focuses on collisionless Cold DM, as it is part of $\Lambda$CDM cosmology, many other forms of DM have been proposed in order to solve known conflicts with this cosmology (see e.g. \citealt{Bullock:2017xww ).}
elements.sent-2411-012: Several methods of detecting DM have been proposed and tried out: from direct detection in a laboratory to indirect detection using signals from astrophysical systems \citep{PDG .}
elements.sent-2411-013: One of these systems is a DM halo with an embedded BH at the centre.
elements.sent-2411-014: For potential detections, the density distributions of these systems must be well understood.
elements.sent-2411-015: This article will numerically simulate DM haloes with a central BH, and contextualise the results using the possible detection of DM through gravitational waves (GWs).
elements.sent-2411-016: There are more detection methods where the halo profile is of great importance, such as the detection of gamma-rays from DM self-annihilation (e.g. \citealt{Gondolo,BertoneGammaRaysIMBH, gammaray ), but this work will only consider the impact on the GW interpretation.}
elements.sent-2411-017: This leads to a dephasing of the generated GWs compared to the vacuum solution, which could be measurable with the next generation of gravitational wave detectors (e.g. \citealt{Eda:2014kra,Yue:2018vtk, Kavanagh:2020cfn, Coogan:2021uqv ).}
elements.sent-2411-018: Recent numerical studies found that this dephasing is both larger than previously predicted, and the DM halo is depleted at a slower rate than anticipated, reinforcing the potential of this method \citep{Mukherjee:2023lzn,Kavanagh:2024lgq .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Future GW detectors such as the LISA gravitational wave detector should not only be able to measure this dephasing, but also differentiate between DM haloes and other environments such as accretion discs \citep{LISAreport,Cole:2022yzw .}
elements.sent-2411-020: LISA will be able to extreme and intermediate mass-ratio inspirals where the central BH has a mass between $10^3$ $\text{M _\odot$ and $10^6$ $\text{M _\odot$ \citep{LISAreport .}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Simulations show the Super Massive BHs in the centres of galaxies are orders of magnitude lighter compared to their surrounding haloes (e.g. \citealt{Bandara_2009,Booth:2009zb ).}
elements.sent-2411-022: Furthermore, numerical simulations of CDM haloes have shown that smaller mass haloes are preferred \citep{Sheth:2001dp , and the haloes around Intermediate Mass BHs are less likely to be perturbed due to galactic effects such as mergers \citep{Zhao:2005zr,BertoneGammaRaysIMBH .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: These IMBHs have been detected in our universe (e.g. \citealt{Farrell:2009uxm,Pasham:2014ybe,2024Natur.631..285H ), and simulations show a significant population could be present in our galaxy \citep{Rashkov:2013uua .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: This study thus treats haloes with masses in the order $10^3 - 10^4$ $\text{M _\odot$, with a central BH below the halo mass.}
elements.sent-2411-025: It has been known for some time that the growth of a BH influences the distribution of matter around it (e.g. \citealt{Quinlan:1994ed ).}
elements.sent-2411-026: They analytically showed that if this growth is adiabatic, an initial distribution of ${\rho \propto r^{-\gamma }$ transforms into a spike of slope ${\gamma_\text{GS = (9-2\gamma)/(4-\gamma)}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: These spikes are predicted to start at one-fifth of the radius of gravitational influence of the central BH \citep{MerrittConferenceProceedings,Merritt .}
elements.sent-2411-028: While DM spikes have been of interest for the detection of DM, previous studies into their shape has predominately been (semi-)analytical.
elements.sent-2411-029: To the best of the authors' knowledge, fully numerical N-body simulations of the formation of these DM spikes have never been published.
elements.sent-2411-030: These allow for important checks of (semi-)analytical formalisms, and should lead to more realistic DM halo density profiles.
elements.sent-2411-031: In this article, we present the first numerical step towards realistic spike density distribution functions, by demonstrating the formation of a DM spike in N-body simulations using realistic Hernquist halo profiles.
elements.sent-2411-032: In Section \ref{sec:theoreticalbackground , we give the used DM halo distribution functions and analytical predictions of the expected spikes.}
elements.sent-2411-033: In Section \ref{sec:numericalmethods , we describe the used code and numerical schemes (Section \ref{sec:codeandnumericalschemes ), how the initial haloes are constructed (Section \ref{sec:initialisationofthehaloes ), and a description of the simulated systems and their processing (Section \ref{sec:simulatedsystemsandprocessing ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: In Section \ref{sec:results , we propose a new profile for the spike (Section \ref{sec:res:spikeprofile ), and contextualise the results by estimating their impact on the detectability of dephasing in GWs (Section \ref{sec:implicationsofresults ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Finally, in Section \ref{sec:conclusions , we draw conclusions and some concluding remarks are given.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Appendices with additional material regarding numerical solutions and validation of the simulations are also supplied after the bibliography.
elements.sent-2411-037: Many different DM density profiles exist and are actively compared to observational data (e.g. \citealt{Li:2020iib ).}
elements.sent-2411-038: Arguably the most prominent spherical profiles are the \citet*{NFW (NFW) and the \citet{Hernquist profiles.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Both feature an inner cusp of $\rho \propto r^{-1}$, with the Hernquist having a more steep outer density falloff of $\rho \propto r^{-4}$ compared to $\rho \propto r^{-3}$ of the NFW.
elements.sent-2411-040: We will use the Hernquist profile, because it does not require the use of artificial cut-offs and is easier to work with analytically.
elements.sent-2411-041: Due to the identical inner cusp we expect the results presented in this article to be equivalent for both profiles.
elements.sent-2411-042: The density profile of a Hernquist halo is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:HernqDensity} \rho_\text{Hernq} = \frac{M_\text{h}}{2\pi}\frac{a}{r}\frac{1}{(r+a)^3},\end{equation} where $M_\text{h}$ is the total mass of the halo, and $a$ is the characteristic scale radius.
elements.sent-2411-043: Its gravitational potential $\psi_\text{Hernq}$ is then given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:HernqPotential} \psi_\text{Hernq} = -\frac{G M_\text{h}}{r + a}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-164: The mass distribution of DM around BHs is of vital importance to some astrophysical DM detection methods.
elements.sent-2411-165: We demonstrated the formation of DM spikes in fully numerical N-body simulations using realistic Hernquist halo profiles.
elements.sent-2411-166: The constant inner slope of $\rho \propto r^{-1}$ means our results should be equivalent for other profiles such as the NFW, especially at low mass ratio $\mu$.
elements.sent-2411-167: We proposed a new profile for these DM spikes, which deviates from theoretical predictions and common approximations.
elements.sent-2411-168: This profile only depends on a single new parameter compared to the initial halo profile, the mass ratio of BH and the total system $\mu$.
elements.sent-2411-169: We observe a depletion of the original Hernquist of up to 20\%, and show the spike radius follows a different dependence on $\mu$.
elements.sent-2411-170: This impacts the overall normalisation of the spike compared to previous assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-171: We constrained the value of $\gamma_\text{sp}$ between a value of $-1.9$ and $-7/3$.
elements.sent-2411-172: We note the theoretical value of $-7/3$ is obtained for systems where the BH mass is approximately one order of magnitude below that of the halo, while the value of $-1.9$ is found in systems where the difference is two orders of magnitude.
elements.sent-2411-173: The impact of these results on future DM detections has been demonstrated using the expected dephasing of gravitational waves due to the presence of a DM halo.
elements.sent-2411-174: This dephasing was shown to deviate between our profile and previous approximations, increasingly so as the spike slope and mass ratio $\mu$ increases.
elements.sent-2411-175: Our study unlocks the potential for future investigations into the exact shape of DM spikes and predictions for future DM detections.
elements.sent-2411-176: An improved resolution would yield smaller errors on the fitted parameters of the proposed profile, especially the slope, and allow for lower $\mu$ systems to be probed.
elements.sent-2411-177: Simply increasing the number of particles is not feasible however, due to the scaling nature of N-body simulations.
elements.sent-2411-178: If achieved however, an exact value for the spike slope can be determined and larger mass differences in the system can be probed, bringing us closer to realistic DM spike profiles.

elements.sent-2411-246: We critically assess approximations of the spike as used in the field, show that our profile significantly deviates, and contextualise the potential influence for future DM detections by simulating binary black hole inspirals embedded in our profile.
